The Weird Parts of JS (No.1): Syntax Parsers, Execution Contexts, and Lexical Environments

I'm kicking off a whole series on the weird parts of JavaScript. The main goal is to really dig into what's often called the most misunderstood programming language in the world — and honestly, I'm hoping that taking these notes helps me grow as a developer too.

Whenever a program runs, there's this thing called a "syntax parser" that helps the computer figure out what you're actually trying to do. The syntax parser is also sometimes called a "compiler," and it's related to an "interpreter" too.
So how does a syntax parser actually work?

instead. And that's exactly why the parser sort of taunts you with an error like this:
Ah ah ah! You didn't say the magic word!

I mentioned the "compiler" earlier — so what the heck is that? The course describes a compiler as a kind of middleman, one whose whole job is to convert the code we write into instructions the computer can understand.

written by a programmer (a JavaScript programmer, in this case). So all the code we've written up to now follows the rules that programmer laid down. Which tells you something: the programmer who designed the compiler gets to decide everything. They could've decided that
instead — totally possible. This is one of the concepts you really need to internalize, because tons of other programming languages share the same idea.
Honestly, the term itself sounds scary and complicated to me too. But all it really means is

Kicking off a whole series on the weird parts of JavaScript — the language people misunderstand more than any other. In this first post I take notes on three foundational ideas: the syntax parser, the lexical environment, and the execution context. They sound scary, but they're not. Let's untangle them.
